text: vv. 1, 2. It is not clear whether the prophet was to draw a map, or simply to write the name, of Jerusalem. The directions in verse 2 may be simply an anticipation of verse 3; the rim of the iron cooking-vessel was to represent the continuous cordon of besiegers.

vv. 4-8. The word rendered ‘to sleep’ in the Latin may also mean ‘to lie’, and it is generally understood that the prophet was to remain all day and all night in the same posture. The figures are extremely perplexing. We should have expected that the days would be equal in number with those of the siege, but this, according to IV Kg. 25, lasted about 500 days. The forty years do not match the interval between the taking of Samaria in 722 and the taking of Jerusalem in 587. And we should expect the other figure to correspond with the number of years between the division of the Kingdom and the fall of Samaria, i.e. about 410 years; instead of which the Hebrew text gives 390 and the Septuagint Greek 190.
The different kinds of crop are probably meant to symbolize the simultaneous deficiency of all bread-stuffs; cf. verse 16.
